sections.title: Document Summary
sections.content:
UNIT–I 
Q1. Illustrate any five of the following terms with a neat sketch 
(a) Confidentiality 
Confidentiality ensures that information is accessible only to authorized users and is not disclosed to 
unauthorized persons. 
Example: Encryption of bank account details during online transactions. 
 
(b) Authentication 
Authentication is the process of verifying the identity of a user or system before allowing access. 
Example: Login using username–password or OTP verification. 
 
(c) Availability 
Availability ensures that systems, services, and data are accessible to authorized users whenever required. 
Example: Banking servers providing service 24×7. 
 
(d) Integrity 
Integrity ensures that data remains accurate, complete, and unaltered during transmission or storage. 
Example: Use of hash functions to detect data modification. 
 
(e) Access Control 

Access control determines who can access system resources and what operations they are permitted to 
perform. 
Example: File permissions such as read, write, and execute. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(f) Non-repudiation 
Non-repudiation ensures that neither the sender nor the receiver can deny having sent or received the 
message. 
Example: Digital signatures in online transactions. 
 
Q2. Define a security attack. Explain various types of attacks with suitable examples 
A security attack is any action that compromises the security of information by violating confidentiality, 
integrity, or availability of data or systems. 
Types of Security Attacks 
Security attacks are broadly classified into Passive attacks and Active attacks. 
1. Passive Attacks 
Passive attacks involve monitoring or listening to data transmission without altering the data. 
(a) Eavesdropping / Snooping 
An attacker secretly listens to communication between sender a

sections.title: Detailed Notes
sections.content:
nd receiver. 
Example: Attacker capturing passwords over an unsecured Wi-Fi network. 
(b) TraƯic Analysis 
Attacker observes communication patterns to gain information. 
Example: Identifying active users by analyzing network traƯic volume. 
2. Active Attacks: Active attacks involve modifying, deleting, or disrupting data or services. 
(a) Masquerade 
An attacker pretends to be an authorized user. 
Example: Using stolen login credentials to access a system. 

(b) Replay Attack 
Valid data is captured and retransmitted later by the attacker. 
Example: Reusing a captured authentication message to gain access. 
(c) Modification of Messages 
Data is altered during transmission. 
Example: Changing transaction amount in online banking. 
(d) Denial of Service (DoS) 
System resources are flooded to make services unavailable. 
Example: Flooding a server with requests to crash it. 
3.Specific Attack Techniques: 
Malware: Malicious software (viruses, worms, ransomware) designed to damage or infiltrate systems. 
Social Engineering (Phishing): Tricking individuals into revealing sensitive information. 
Man-in-the-Middle (MITM): Intercepting communication between two parties. 
SQL Injection: Inserting malicious code into web applications to manipulate databases. 
Q3. Define a security attack and explain the concepts of interruption, interception, modification, and 
fabrication 
A security attack is any action that attempts to compromise the security of information by violating 
confidentiality, integrity, or availability. 
Types of Security Attacks 
 
1. Interruption 
Interruption occurs when system resources or data become unavailable to authorized users. 
 Security goal aƯected: Availability 
 Example: Server crash or Denial of Service (DoS) attack 
Sender ──X──► Receiver 
(System

sections.title: Document Summary
sections.content:
Crypto Volatility and Risk Analyzer 
 
 
Project Statement 
The project “Crypto Volatility and Risk Analyzer” aims to analyze and visualize the volatility 
patterns of selected cryptocurrencies to estimate their risk levels over time. The system fetches 
real-time and historical cryptocurrency data from APIs such as CoinGecko or Binance and 
performs quantitative analysis to measure volatility, Sharpe ratio, and beta coefficients. 
Using data analytics and visualization libraries in Python (like Matplotlib and Plotly), this project 
will generate interactive visual dashboards that display risk insights. The tool will ultimately help 
traders, investors, and researchers identify high-risk and low-risk cryptocurrencies through 
dynamic and data-driven visual analysis. 
 
Outcomes 
1. Dynamic Data Fetching: 
Automatically fetch live and historical cryptocurrency data using APIs (Binance / 
CoinGecko). 

2. Risk Analysis Metrics: 
Compute financial indicators including daily and annualized volatility, Sharpe ratio, and 
beta coefficients. 
3. Interactive Visualizations: 
Build real-time visual dashboards using Plotly or Matplotlib to depict price trends and 
volatility movement. 
4. Risk Categorization: 
Classify cryptocurrencies into High-Risk, Medium-Risk, and Low-Risk groups based on 
computed metrics. 
5. Decision Support: 
Provide actionable insights for investors by visually comparing volatility and return 
trade-offs. 
 
Modules to be Implemented 
1. Data Acquisition Module 
o Fetch live and historical price data for multiple cryptocurrencies. 
o Handle missing values and format data for analysis. 
o Data source integration (CoinGecko, Binance). 
2. Data Processing and Calculation Module 
o Compute daily returns, volatility, Sharpe ratio, beta coefficient, and Value-

sections.title: Detailed Notes
sections.content:
at-Risk 
(VaR). 
o Apply rolling window methods to track time-based variations. 
3. Visualization Module 
o Create interactive graphs such as line charts, scatter plots, and risk-return maps 
using Plotly or Matplotlib. 
o Display comparative volatility trends across multiple assets. 
4. Risk Classification and Reporting Module 
o Categorize cryptocurrencies based on volatility and Sharpe ratio thresholds. 
o Generate summarized reports with key insights and visual exports. 
 

Week-wise Module Implementation and High-Level Requirements 
Milestone 1 – Week 1 & Week 2: Data Acquisition and Setup 
Requirements: 
• Set up project environment and libraries (pandas, numpy, requests, plotly, dash). 
• Integrate APIs (Binance/CoinGecko) for fetching crypto price data. 
• Store fetched data into local storage (CSV/Parquet format). 
• Perform basic preprocessing – missing value handling and time-series formatting. 
Output: 
• Successfully retrieved and stored daily price data for at least 5 cryptocurrencies (e.g., 
BTC, ETH, SOL, ADA, DOGE). 
• Verified API connectivity and data consistency. 
 
 
Milestone 2 – Week 3 & Week 4: Data Processing and Calculation 
Requirements: 
• Calculate daily returns using log-return formulas. 
• Derive statistical measures: 

o Daily and annualized volatility 
o Sharpe ratio 
o Beta coefficient (vs benchmark like BTC). 
• Implement moving average and rolling window volatility. 
Output: 
• Generated a metrics table with volatility, Sharpe ratio, and beta values for all selected 
assets. 
• Prepared intermediate dataset for visualization and risk classification. 
 
 
Milestone 3 – Week 5 & Week 6: Visualization and Dashboard Development 
Requirements: 
• Build interactive visualizations using Plotly or Matplotlib. 
• Develop an analytical dashboar

sections.title: Document Summary
sections.content:
CLOUD COMPUTING 
Cloud computing is the delivery of computing services like storage, servers, networking, 
databases, and software over the internet on a pay-as-you-use basis. 
Users do not need to manage physical hardware or software infrastructure. 
Problems with Traditional IT Architecture 
Traditional IT systems face several limitations: 
• High cost for data centres and hardware 
• Maintenance, cooling, and power expenses 
• Limited scalability 
• Location dependency 
• Difficult disaster recovery 
Why Cloud Computing? 
Cloud computing solves traditional IT problems by offering: 
• No upfront investment 
• On-demand resource availability 
• Easy scalability 
• Reduced operational cost 
• High availability and disaster recovery 
Key Features of Cloud Computing 
• High Scalability – Resources can be increased or decreased easily 
• Flexibility – Access services anytime, anywhere 
• Cost Reduction – No need to buy infrastructure 
• High Security – Advanced security provided by vendors 
• High Availability – 99.99% uptime 
• Disaster Recovery – Fast recovery from failures 
Cloud Deployment Models 
A cloud deployment model defines who owns the cloud, who can access it, and how secure it is. 
Types of Cloud Deployment Models: 
1. Public Cloud 
2. Private Cloud 
3. Community Cloud 
4. Hybrid Cloud 

 
 
1. Public Cloud Model 
Public Cloud resources are owned and managed by third-party providers and delivered via the 
internet. 
Key Characteristics: 
• Pay-per-use model 
• Highly scalable 
• No infrastructure management 
• Suitable for large-scale applications 
Advantages of Public Cloud: 
• No infrastructure management 
• Highly scalable 
• Cost-effective 
• High availability 
• Ideal for startups and testing environments 
Examples: Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Az

sections.title: Detailed Notes
sections.content:
ure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), IBM 
Cloud, Oracle Cloud 
2. Private Cloud Model 
Private Cloud is used by a single organization and offers high security. 
Limitations: Expensive, Requires maintenance, Limited scalability 
3. Community Cloud Model 
Shared by organizations with similar requirements (e.g., banks, universities). 
Benefits: 
• Cost sharing 
• Better security than public cloud 
4. Hybrid Cloud Model 
Combination of Public + Private Cloud. 
Advantages: 
• High flexibility 
• Better security 
• Scalable and reliable
